text: Apex Corruption Erodes Democratic Values * Eduardo Rivera Enrique Seira Saumitra Jha 2025-05-06 Abstract We complement a field experiment with cross-country evidence to show that exposure to apex corruption — corrupt acts implicating top-level politicians—causes large decreases in democratic values and associated behaviors.
Behaviors such as individual voter turnout, contributions to support elections, and honesty and trust in incentivized games all fall.
The effects on voting are greatest for incumbents perceived to be honest when apex corruption is exposed close to elections.
We show, both experimentally and across scandals in 17 national settings, that apex corruption also reduces explicit support for democracy in favor of authoritarianism.
We test two restorative solutions.
JEL: D72, D73, C93, C72, D02, D91, K42, O10, P00, Z1 Keywords: apex corruption, democratic backsliding, institutions, norms, trust, voting.

"Corruption is the poison of democracy” Mario Vargas Llosa
Democratic institutions, once believed to be stable, now face increasing challenges in many countries around the world.
Political parties avowedly skeptical of democratic institutions have been admitted to ruling coalitions in a quarter of countries.

The willingness of citizens to stand up for democratic institutions is often seen as a key driver of democratic resilience, but both globally and in Latin America, citizens’ dissatisfaction with democracy has increased by 10–15 percentage points since the mid-1990s.
Diagnosing the underlying causes of this crisis of support for democracy is crucial for uncovering solutions to reverse it.
While democratic values are eroding, citizens perceive that corruption is widespread, particularly as high-profile cases implicate top-level politicians.
In Latin America, the share of people who say corruption is their country’s most important problem has more than doubled, from 4% in 2010 to 10% in 2018.
Latin America is not alone.

The United States, too, has seen dramatic falls in democratic satisfaction, while the share of citizens that claim that corruption is widespread in government has risen to rival that of Latin America (Figure 1 ).
Could these macro patterns be causal?
Could exposure to apex corruption — corrupt acts implicating apex (top-level) politicians—be undermining democratic values?
In this paper, we complement a field experiment in Mexico with evidence from apex corruption scandals in 17 countries and show that the answer is yes.
On one hand, the spotlighting and exposure of corruption at any level is often regarded as contributing to a healthier democracy.

Indeed, as we describe below, a thriving literature, focused almost exclusively on local political and bureaucratic corruption, has emphasized the important role that can be played by random audits, political watchdogs, opposition groups and a free media in fostering increased transparency and political accountability.

Yet, revealing apex corruption could also have underappreciated downsides that may be particularly destructive of the complementary organizations, internalized norms, and beliefs that comprise liberal democratic institutions. 1 This is because, despite being often more physically remote from the average citizen, apex politicians differ from local politicians in three key ways that can make them more impactful at a societal level.
First, they have a heightened ability to shape state organizations and influence policymaking directly.
Thus apex malfeasance may be more likely to discredit state organizations, including those that are meant to prevent corruption and support electoral fairness.
Second, apex politicians are more likely to act as focal role models with the potential to influence social norms.
Third, citizens may also change their beliefs, inferring from the success of apex politicians that the system and society tolerate such corrupt practices.
The prominence of apex politicians also naturally implies that there is heightened knowledge of their corrupt acts.
Thus, scandals involving apex politicians are more likely to have an impact on the beliefs of individuals about the pervasiveness of corruption more generally.
In environments where corruption involves strategic complementarities: i.e. that one’s incentives to cheat increases with one’s belief that others do the same (see e.g.

Mauro; Klašnja et al. ), the stronger resultant effects on beliefs can lead not only to decreases in support for democratic systems of government in general, they may further manifest themselves in greater incentives to cheat, reduced trust in other citizens, and may even undermine internalized norms.
All these features can make revelations of apex corruption have large and often underappreciated negative impacts on democratic institutions.
In this paper, we provide direct causal evidence that apex corruption undermines the norms, beliefs, and organizations that underpin liberal democratic institutions.
We employ two complementary strategies to do so.
First, we implement a randomized controlled trial in Mexico prior to the 2021 federal congressional elections that allows us to directly measure the effects of apex corruption.

We complement this with cross-country evidence exploiting the exact timing of the breaking of apex corruption scandals in 17 countries between 2008 to 2018, to establish whether the effects on values generalize.

In our first strategy, we implement a large-scale randomized control trial with over 3,300 citizens that we recruited door-to-door from both 69 rural municipalities and the capital city of Oaxaca in Mexico.
This setting provides a valuable environment to test the effects of apex corruption and the mechanisms through which it operates.
Oaxaca is internally highly diverse, encompassing both traditional and modern institutions of governance, and by itself, includes 570 of Mexico’s 2476 municipalities.
Further, like numerous other settings, Oaxaca, and Mexico more broadly, had recently exhibited a well-spring of support for the populist incumbent president, Andres Manuel López Obrador (AMLO).

In common with many other recent populists, AMLO had won election in 2018 as an anti-corruption crusader, campaigning on an economic nationalist platform against elite corruption following a wave of scandals implicating then-president, Enrique Pẽna Nieto (EPN) and other apex politicians.
Specifically, our basic experiment compares a control group against a set of two apex corruption treatments.

Citizens were randomly assigned 3-minute videos which provided real footage of apex politicians 1 Here we build on Greif ’s definition of institutions as a system of (i) organizations, (ii) beliefs, and (iii) norms that together induce a regularity of behavior.
In our case, the regularities of behaviors we study reflect active support for the democratic process, including voting.
We develop outcome measures corresponding to each of these three components of democratic institutions below.
exchanging stacks of cash either a week or a month before the 2021 elections.

One of those videos —which we call the incumbent corruption video — features one brother of the incumbent president, and the other —the opposition video — shows leading members of the two main opposition parties.

Given strong differences in citizens’ prior beliefs about the prevalence of malfeasance at the top of the new anti-corruption incumbent party compared to leaders of the opposition, we pre-specified that we expected that evidence of apex incumbent corruption would have greater effects on undermining democratic values. 2 Indeed, we show that apex corruption, particularly when implicating the incumbent, undermines a range of pro-democratic behaviors.

Most importantly, by a special legal arrangement with the Mexican electoral authority, we gained unusual access to administrative data on the core mechanism by which citizens hold their leaders accountable in democracies: the individual decision to turnout to vote.
We show that evidence of apex corruption decreases actual voting in Federal Congressional elections by 4 pp to 6. pp on average (or 6.7%–11.6%).
In fact, the effect on actual voting was a remarkable 22. pp (or 39%) for those exposed to incumbent apex corruption a week before the elections.
Further, we establish that apex corruption results in falls across a range of other pro-democratic behaviors measured immediately after treatment as well.

The incumbent apex corruption treatment decreases a citizen’s willingness to donate time to support the organizations of democracy—by volunteering to be an electoral observer—by 43% (relative to the 4.9% who volunteered on average).
It similarly reduces willingness to donate money —10 pesos to buy bottled water for poll-booth workers—by 16% (relative to 22.8% who donated).

Beyond these real-world behaviors, we employ incentivized lab-in-the-field games among a randomized subset of participants to show that apex incumbent corruption also undermines the other components of liberal democratic institutions, including beliefs in the trustworthiness of politicians and neighbors, and internalized norms of honest dealings as well.
Apex incumbent corruption decreases the average amounts entrusted to anonymous politicians and neighbors in trust games by 11.2% and 5.4% respectively.
Further, despite high levels of prevailing honesty overall, treated individuals exhibit increased willingness to steal in a resource allocation game with no possibility of punishment.
They steal 4. 4% more coins when the rest would be donated to their municipal mayor for public projects, and (weakly) 2. 6% more from an anonymous neighbor as well.
The randomized control trial allows us to implement additional treatments to shed further light on the mechanism and potential solutions.
Pervasive corruption often correlates with poor policy decisions and economic underperformance, which could also undermine support for democracy.
To distinguish these channels, we randomly assigned a three-minute video detailing Mexico’s poor economic and policy performance, but without providing any explicit evidence of apex corruption.
We find that, in striking contrast to the apex corruption treatments, economic underperformance increases the probability an individual will vote (by 8–10pp, or 13%–17%).
Like the apex incumbent corruption treatment, the economic underperformance treatment also lowers incumbent voter support substantially if received a week before the election (20.1pp).
However, unlike the apex corruption treatments, this fall is compensated for disproportionately by an increased propensity to turnout and vote for other parties.

In further contrast to apex incumbent corruption, the economic under-performance treatment has no effect on willingness to donate time or money to support democratic 2 Please see our pre-analysis plan (PAP) available on the AEA registry: AEARCTR-0007770.
See also Arias et al.
institutions, though it does raise the propensities to steal opportunistically and to distrust.
We interpret these effects as consistent with apex corruption being distinct in important ways from (potentially well-intentioned) policy ineptitude and economic underperformance.

While both undermine societal trust and may make theft from public projects more tempting, information on economic ineffectiveness spurs voters’ willingness to turnout and hold politicians accountable by registering protest votes.
In contrast, the revelation of actual apex corrupt activity has more severe and pervasive effects on decreasing support for democratic institutions and undermining pro-democratic behaviors.

Our intention in the field experiment was to diagnose the causal effects of informing individuals about apex corruption and begin to uncover how we can mitigate its potential ill-effects and strengthen democratic institutions.
Therefore we also test two potentially promising and readily scalable remedies for rebuilding societal trust: common identity and common financial exposures.

As our first potential solution, we commissioned a 3-minute nation-building video from a professional media designer to prime common national identity and how far Mexicans have gone to build democratic institutions.

Indeed, in the United States, a video priming common national identity was one of the most effective of 25 different treatments at reducing individuals’ willingness to express support for undemocratic practices, see also Levendusky; Rohner and Zhuravskaya; Esposito et al.
The nation-building treatment was appreciated for its high quality, but contrary to our expectations, did not broadly improve pro-democratic behaviors.

It had no average effect on voter turnout, and instead, it led to behaviors that are consistent with greater alignment with the populist and nationalist messaging of the incumbent president—lowering votes for the main opposition parties (to the benefit of the incumbent, p-value 0.13), and decreasing time and money donations to the electoral authority that the incumbent president vocally criticized.

As a second solution—common financial exposure—we gave a subset of individuals the opportunity to invest in the national economy by giving them 200 pesos of financial assets tracking a broad Mexican stock index, and allowing them to trade up to 25 pesos each week. 3 Shared financial exposure yielded encouraging (though suggestive) evidence of positive effects on democratic values and trust that offset some of the negative impacts of apex corruption.
We then turn to further probing the mechanisms and generalizability of our findings.

One natural question that arises is whether the changes in voting and other pro-democratic behaviors that we observe in our RCT reflects changes in democratic values and other attitudes that individuals perceive.
A second natural question is whether the effects of apex corruption persist over time and whether they cumulate in response to new evidence of corrupt activity.

A third question is whether the causal patterns we observe in Mexico generalize to other environments where individuals also just happened to be naturally exposed to scandals involving apex politicians.
We shed new light on each of these in turn.

To unpack the first mechanism, we conduct our own novel surveys using pre-specified indices of perceptions of corruption, support for democracy, and trust in democratic institutions and other citizens more generally. 4 We demonstrate that the changes in political behaviors due to apex corruption are indeed reflected in changes in values of which citizens are aware.

Unlike a number of studies, which found that providing leaflets with audit reports on local officials had very limited or no effect on corruption 3 Jha and Shayo show that a stronger intervention among participants in an internet panel in Israel changed the political attitudes of individuals, and in other settings it increased generalized trust and led individuals to resist populist narratives. 4 The indices we use to measure these effects were prespecified in our pre-analysis plan - see AEARCTR-0007770.

perceptions, randomized exposure to video evidence of apex corruption raises corruption perceptions by 0. standard deviations ( %, a 5% decrease in the probability individuals believe democracy to be the best form of government, and 4.7% decrease that it is essential to live in a democracy.

Similarly, trust in the President decreases by 10%, in Congress by 23% and in political parties by 19.7%. Further, as with the behavioral measures, the corrosive effect of apex corruption is not confined to the political domain.
It spills over to undermine stated trust in other citizens and in the media as well.

Again consistent with our pre-registered hypotheses, the effects tend to be more marked among those who were ex ante more supportive of the anti-corruption incumbent party, whose leadership was widely perceived as exemplifying apex honesty (see Figure OA-9 ). 5 We next test whether the effects persist and even accumulate.

Using a follow-up survey four months post-treatment, we find that there are persistent effects of apex corruption on undermining support for democracy: though corruption perceptions among the treatment and control groups converge over time, the undermining of democratic values not only persists, it also accumulates in the presence of further evidence of apex corruption.
We exploit a second apex corruption scandal that broke during our study period involving a second brother of the incumbent president.

We find that when respondents are exposed to new evidence of not just one but two brothers of the incumbent president taking bribes, the undermining of support for democracy becomes even more pronounced among individuals previously exposed to treatment in the first wave. 6 We finally confirm the external validity of our RCT results by examining large-scale survey responses in 17 Latin American countries from 2008 to 2018 that happened to be conducted a few days before and a few days after an apex corruption scandal, using measures analogous to those in our RCT. We find that relative to otherwise similar respondents surveyed just before a corruption scandal, those surveyed just afterward reveal both a 0. %. These results are not driven by general pessimism or changes in views on economic underperformance: while those surveyed after an apex corruption scandal are 30% more likely to perceive corruption to be the main problem faced by the country, their satisfaction with the economy and non-democratic related institutions do not change.
Instead, as in our RCT, apex corruption scandals appear to specifically undermine support for democratic values and democratic institutions.
This study contributes to our understanding in several ways.

First, it complements other prominent explanations for democratic backsliding, which include financial crises and economic stagnation, austerity policies and government ineffectiveness, and the rise of social media and polarization.

While a large literature in economics studies corruption, including its extent ( Svensson, 2003; Reinikka 5 The effects on perceptions of corruption are higher for those exposed to evidence of incumbent corruption (0.

and Svensson, 2004 ), sources, organization, detection, and policies to fight it, to the best of our knowledge, ours is the only study that causally estimates the effects of apex corruption scandals on democratic behaviors and values.

As discussed above, conceptually, increased transparency about apex corruption could increase support for democracy, as it may demonstrate that even the acts of even the most powerful politicians are being scrutinized and thus they can be held to account.
We show, however, that the opposite is true.

By focusing on the relationship between apex corruption and democratic values, our paper adds a new dimension to an important literature that examines the effects of information disclosures about local governmental performance on local political accountability.
Seminal work includes those by Ferraz and Finan and Chong et al.

Consistent with the accountability channel, Ferraz and Finan show that following randomized audits of local government expenditures, those municipalities that showed significant irregularities and were covered by the media were less likely to re-elect municipal mayors in Brazil.

In contrast, in Mexico, Chong et al. randomize the distribution of flyers that report the size of the municipality budget and the fraction that “did not comply with the [legal] norms” according to official government audits.
They find the flyer had no effect on perceptions of corruption but estimate a subsequent modest decrease of 1. pp in municipal election turnout.

Newer work by Arias et al. finds that the effect of such audit information depends importantly on the extent to which these audits are a surprise to voters given their existing priors, with high malfeasance/misallocation surprises particularly increasing turnout in local elections.

More generally, however, the Metaketa studies perform a coordinated multi-country RCT providing information to voters, and find “ no evidence overall that typical, nonpartisan voter information campaigns shape voter behavior. ” They attribute this to the information not having a discernible effect on perceptions of politicians’ integrity, as audit-type information treatments are often hard to understand, and not treated as particularly meaningful in light of individuals’ pre-existing beliefs about corruption levels.
We complement these works and advance the literature in a number of ways.

Fundamentally, our paper distinguishes itself from the literature both in terms of the treatment of interest— apex corruption rather than local corruption or accounting irregularities—and in terms of outcomes studied— directly measuring effects on democratic values, support for institutions, and a range of political behaviors.
But beyond this, we advance this work with a number of substantive and methodological contributions.
Among these, we are able to exploit administrative data on individual voter turnout that allows us to circumvent both potential social desirability and ecological inference concerns.

We employ a more visceral vehicle of treatment—easy to understand videos showing direct footage of corrupt activity rather than pamphlets of audit reports—that actually change both corruption perceptions as well as behaviors.
Further, we are able to causally distinguish the effects of apex corruption from (potentially well-intentioned) policy mis-management and underperformance.
Beyond this too, we are able to use the RCT approach to experimentally study both the decay of the effect over time and the cumulative effects of multiple scandals.
Moreover, we can assess the generality of these effects using a natural experiment evidence from 17 countries.
Finally, we evaluate the effects of two promising remedies emerging from the recent (but mostly parallel) nation-building and democratic backsliding literature.

In these ways, our work also relates to two important recent papers that do connect cross-country causes for democratic backsliding and the rise of populism with economic underperformance and average societal corruption levels.
Guriev et al. exploit the rollout of 3G technology to show that places that gained access to mobile internet saw falls in support for incumbents, with populists the main beneficiaries.
The average level of corruption in society was an important mediating factor, with 3G actually raising incumbent support only in the cleanest societies (i.e.
Denmark and Switzerland).

Relatedly, Acemoglu et al. use a difference-in-difference approach to show that individuals living for longer periods of time in ‘successful’ democracies—including states that have performed well economically and effectively controlled corruption—are more supportive of democracy.
However, they find that the flipside: the relationship between those living longer in ‘unsuccessful’ democracies (including more corrupt ones) and democratic support is less clear.
Our paper also closely relates to an important emerging literature on the contagion of local corruption.
Ajzenman studies the publication of results of municipal expenditure audits and their effects on cheating in exams by secondary school students.

Using a differences-in-differences strategy, his study compares municipalities-years incurring unauthorized uses of funds versus those that did not, and finds that following revelations of corruption by local officials, secondary students’ cheating on cognitive tests increases.

As a validation, the paper implements a differences-in-differences strategy exploiting the timing of the audit release and finds that survey respondents report lower civic values such as honesty, rule abidance, trustworthiness, among others.
In a similar vein, Gulino and Masera compares supermarket theft before and after local corruption scandals in municipalities with and without scandals in two Italian provinces.
They find that the probability of stealing increases by 16% after a local corruption scandal breaks.

We contribute to this literature by showing that these effects are not limited to local malfeasance: in fact, exposure of corruption among national politicians at the highest levels can also influence citizens’ propensities to steal, and importantly extend beyond everyday forms of ‘retail’ dishonesty to durably undermine democratic values and institutions more generally.

In doing so, we provide evidence consistent with theoretical contributions pointing out how leaders and prominent figures can affect culture and institutions, as well as expectations and social norms, and countrylevel outcomes.

Our paper adds to a growing body of empirical work demonstrating that individual leaders and central figures in hierarchical networks can not only shape organizational performance and efficacy of public policy campaigns, but can even have macro-level political and economic consequences.

Finally, having established the effects of apex corruption, we also innovate by taking steps to assess countervailing treatments, including the effects of making shared national identity salient and shared financial exposure.
We find some reason for hope.
Context Examples of apex corruption are unfortunately all-too-common, and can involve very substantial sums.
For example, in 2019, a single company ( Odebrecht ) was disclosed to have paid almost $ 1 billion in bribes in order to obtain contracts.
Alleged recipients include apex politicians such as Brazilian President Michel Temer, the vice-president of Ecuador, Jorge Glas, among others.

In Peru a special set of prison cells was constructed to house four ex-presidents implicated in the scandal. 7 Perhaps not surprisingly then, citizens’ perceptions of the pervasiveness of corruption have also risen to high levels: 68% of citizens in Latin America in 2018 believed that half or more of their politicians are corrupt.

References to corruption have doubled in English and tripled in Spanish language news outlets since 2000 (Figure OA-1 ). 8 In Mexico and Latin America, more generally, satisfaction with democracy has fallen while perceptions that corruption is the main problem of society have more than doubled (Figure OA-2 ).
Latin America is far from alone.

The Panama Papers implicated 140 prominent politicians in 50 countries, spanning both rich and developing nations. 9 Even in a country often perceived to be in a low-corruption equilibrium like the United States, the leading presidential candidates in each of the 2016, 2020 and 2024 election cycles (or close family members) have faced allegations and investigations for corruption.

Deepening concerns about such apex corruption have been accompanied with a populist wave in Latin America and elsewhere, with political figures gaining traction with promises “ to drain the swamp ”, i.e. seeking national renewal at the expense of existing democratic institutions seen as corrupt and compromised.
A useful example of this more global pattern is Andres Manuel López Obrador (AMLO), Mexico’s incumbent president at the time of our study.
AMLO ran his 2018 presidential campaign on a nationalist populist platform, with a strong anti-corruption message.

So much so that leading national and international media observers dubbed AMLO an “ anti-corruption crusader ”. 10 Deeply critical of existing institutions of government (“ Al diablo con sus instituciones [to hell with you and your institutions]!” ), AMLO pledged to use savings from eliminating corruption to finance social policies and fight poverty, and founded a new party: Morena — the Movement for National Regeneration. 11 This approach appears to have resonated with many voters, who voted for him in a landslide.
AMLO’s decisive victory was arguably a strong rejection by voters of continued apex corruption in which many would likely implicate AMLO’s predecessor as president, Enrique Peña Nieto.
Nieto was alleged to have directed millions in bribes while in office.

Instead AMLO has cultivated an image of apex honesty by using commercial aircraft, driving a cheap car, and constantly repeating his motto “ no somos iguales ” [we are not the same] and his campaign slogan “ honestidad valiente ” [courageous honesty]. Both voting behavior and official national surveys suggest that people’s beliefs both in the probity of the person of the president and the prospect of lower institutional corruption did indeed change.

AMLO won election by more than 30 percentage points, and between 2017 (the year before he took office) and 2019 (one year after he took office), there was a 21 percentage point drop in the fraction of people who 7 See: “ Odebrecht case: politicians world-wide suspected in bribery scandal ”, BBC News, 17 April 2019. 8 Alternative measures of retail corruption, including bribe paying by firms and reports by experts have also increased (Figures OA-3 and OA-4 ), suggesting the media reports may not be entirely divorced from on-the-ground practice.
Yet, though such heightened perceptions correlate with actual corrupt behaviors, they are often far from perfect.
Our empirical strategy however does not hinge on the accuracy of corruption perceptions.

As we noted above, the experiment shows citizens evidence of actual apex corruption and tests its effects. 9 See “ The Power Players: Politicians in the Panama Papers, International Consortium of Investigative Journalists, 2016. 10 “ Mexico’s new president is a nationalist, but he’s not anti-American”; “ López Obrador, an an anti-corruption crusader, has dominated polls this election season, riding a wave of anger about government corruption. ”. “ The left-wing anti-establishment candidate, Andrés Manuel López Obrador, will make tackling corruption his top priority after winning Mexico’s presidential election on Sunday ” (BBC). “ Mexico’s new leader is riding a wave of anti-corruption furor that’s changing Latin America”, and that ” The new president insists that his anti-corruption campaign —including a 50-point plan to fight corruption and reduce privileges for officials — will be transformative. ”. 11 " If you ask me to express in one sentence what the new government’s plan is all about: end corruption and impunity.".
believe the Mexican presidency to be corrupt. 12 Indeed, in our baseline survey, only 4% considered the incumbent party to be more corrupt than the opposition.

This may have further given AMLO latitude to sow distrust and threaten legislative action against the well-regarded independent electoral agency (INE), among other institutions. 13 AMLO’s movement both provides an intriguing case in which a single leader appears to have had significant influence in shaping corruption perceptions and support for democratic institutions in society, and also a valuable empirical environment to test for differential effects when high expectations related to apex honesty are not met.

We thus pre-specified that evidence of apex corruption involving the anticorruption Morena party would have stronger effects than evidence of corruption involving opposition parties, as the latter were already widely considered to be compromised.
We implemented a field experiment involving 3,331 adult Mexican citizens in Oaxaca, located in the south of Mexico.

Oaxaca is not only one of Mexico’s largest states, with 570 municipalities out of Mexico’s 2471, but also highly diverse, encompassing both traditional indigenous and contemporary institutions of governance.

In 2021, Oaxaca also ranked 6th out of 32 States in Mexico in corruption perceptions according to Mexicanos Contra la Corrupcion y la Impunidad, a prominent NGO. Not surprisingly then, AMLO’s anticorruption message also resonated strongly among Oaxaqueños: 65% voted for Morena, 11% for PAN, 17% for PRI in 2018, the year AMLO was elected.
Within Oaxaca, 41% of the sample was allocated to the capital, Oaxaca City, while the rest was allocated across 69 rural municipalities (map in Figure OA-6 ). 14 3.1

The main objectives of the experiment were to estimate the causal effects of informing adult Mexican citizens of apex corruption, by providing evidence of actual corrupt activity by both (1) the incumbent and (2) the opposition; to (3) separate the apex corruption channel from that of policy ineffectiveness and economic underperformance; and (4) to then begin to test treatments that could strengthen democratic institutions.

Because we wanted the information to be credible and impactful and to communicate it in a simple, homogeneous and scalable way, the treatments involved video footage of politicians exchanging and counting large and illegal stacks of cash which we randomly assigned to participants on electronic tablets (see links below).

Video recording of apex corruption is not uncommon in Latin America, sometimes even by the perpetrators themselves, and as a result apex corruption scandals often break and are disseminated through such footage.
We anticipated that such videos would also be more persuasive than flyers based on audits, which tend to be more technical and have shown mixed and smaller effects.

Indeed, 90% of survey participants believed the veracity of the video evidence. 12 A well-regarded survey in Mexico showed after the 2018 election 52% of citizens thought corruption would decrease with AMLO vs 22% that thought it would increase (Reforma). 13 See e.g.

Anne Applebaum, “How Do You Stop Lawmakers From Destroying the Law?”, the Atlantic, March 1, 2023. 14 Municipalities were stratified to ensure we have several with usos y custombres - traditional indigenous governance institutions and on weather suitability for growing cochineal, an exogenous drive for indigenous identity and culture (see Diaz-Cayeros et al. ), which we study in a companion project.
We designed four videos based on the four objectives above. 15 The videos were implemented as part of a survey.

Each day, a team of enumerators would go to election precincts we selected at random, and they would knock on doors explaining that we came from academic institutions and were conducting a survey lasting approximately 30 minutes.
Respondents received 50 pesos for participating and could receive more from behavioral games at the end of the survey.
The survey was conducted on a high-resolution Samsung tablet.
In the middle of the survey, respondents were randomly allocated to videos and were told they would see a video.
All participants then watched the videos, which were each 3 minutes in duration.
The survey continued after watching the video, enabling us to measure the immediate outcomes that we describe below.
Apex Corruption: We designed two apex corruption videos.

The first part of the video contains official statistics on the fraction of procurement contracts that are assigned directly without a bidding process (explaining that this practice facilitates corruption), and official data on the amount of bribes citizens report paying as part of bureaucratic extortion to obtain permits and pay for government services. 16 Nearly three-fourths of contracts, even large ones, are assigned directly with no bidding, and close to half of Mexicans report having paid a bribe when pursuing bureaucratic procedures.
The second part of the videos had two different versions.
The Incumbent Corruption Video (CI) provides footage of the brother of the incumbent president (AMLO) taking bribes.

The Opposition Corruption Video (CO) similarly shows footage of prominent politicians from the main opposition party (PAN) taking large quantities of money, as well as testimony from a politician from the other major opposition party (PRI) about large bribes taken by PRI cabinet members.
Economic underperformance: This video also lasts close to 3 minutes.
There is no mention of corruption.
Instead it describes the performance of the three last Mexican governments (PAN, PRI, Morena) in three dimensions: poverty, economic growth, and government relief during COVID-19.

Using official data from Mexico’s CONEVAL, CEPAL, and the World Bank, it shows that 1 in every 4 Mexicans are in poverty and that this has stayed almost constant since 2000, while Chile, Panama, and Uruguay have performed much better.
In terms of economic growth, Guatemala, Paraguay, Peru, the Dominican Republic, and Uruguay had grown more than twice as much as Mexico from 2010 to 2019.

Finally, it shows that Mexico had the 3rd worst place in terms of COVID-related deaths to that point and that the government invested less than 0.7% of GDP on COVID relief, which is several times smaller than the world at large.
Nation-Building: We hired a renowned media designer to present an emotive national narrative in a video reminding Mexicans of their shared identity and rich culture.

The video encompasses the formation of the country from the Aztecs and Mayans to contemporaneous Mexican culture, humor, food, music, the flag and family values, as well as revered poets and heroes, Mexican fortitude and union in the face of natural disasters, and the long and effortful path to obtaining democracy.
The video was motivated by an emerging literature (see Rohner and Zhuravskaya ) showing that national narratives delivered in movie format can affect national identity.

Indeed, videos priming 15 Links: Incumbent corruption video: https://youtu.be/BSdueriPSA4; Opposition video: https://youtu.be/HyLrEmvzqPQ; Economic video: https://youtu.be/6Y8EM9uGmwE; Nation-building video: https://youtu.be/-fE_UgbDl. 16 The data is from INEGI’s Encuesta Nacional de Calidad e Impacto Gubernamental 2017 and 2019, and includes obtaining permits (e.g. to open a firm, to build, to obtain a driver’s license), paying for services (e.g. electricity, water, pay taxes), requesting a government loan, using the courts, among others.
We had piloted this information before and found that coming from official statistics, citizens found it credible and perceived it as non-partisan.
shared national identity have been among the most effective in countering anti-democratic attitudes in recent mega-studies in the United States.
We similarly conjectured that the nationbuilding narrative could increase trust, foster pro-social behaviors and support for democratic institutions.
Randomization We randomize 3,331 respondents into main 5 experimental arms, corresponding to the 4 videos above plus a control group with no video.
Figure OA-7 provides a timeline of the experiment and Table OA-1 the samples across arms.
Randomization was done at the individual survey-folio level and loaded into the tablets the day before the fieldwork started.
It was blind to the enumerator and the respondent, with a notification message appearing on the tablet just at the moment the video treatment module was to start.
The enumerator then ensured the respondents saw the video.
After the video treatment, the survey continued, and we measured short-run effects.
Timing randomization.

For the urban sample in the city of Oaxaca (41% of participants) we also randomized at the electoral precinct level when respondents were interviewed and exposed to the videos. 1055 individuals in 27 precincts were allocated to receive the videos about a month (3–5 weeks) before the election, while 311 individuals in 10 precincts received it about a week (5–8 days) before the election.
This randomization allows us to measure the durability of the effect on voting, which has proven an enduring challenge for survey experiments in this domain.
To our knowledge only Gerber et al. have randomized timing of—in their case—political TV ads during electoral campaigns.
Financial exposure treatment.

For the urban sample in Oaxaca city, where continued communications could be conducted via text message, we implemented a treatment where citizens were assigned 200 pesos (about $10 USD), of which they could trade a portion weekly in the main Mexican index fund, similar in spirit to Jha and Shayo.
This was implemented after the immediate survey outcomes were recorded and thus only impacts longer-term outcomes.
Experimental integrity.
One advantage of the survey experiment is they typically yield negligible attrition.
Among those who started the survey, 100% assigned to the video saw the video and finished the remainder of the survey.
That is, we have no attrition, and of course no differential attrition by arm.
Randomization also achieved balance.
Table OA-2 shows balance for our main results sample, while Table OA-3 shows balance for the timing randomization.
We employ Ordinary Least Squares to estimate the following equation:

indicators of individual voter turnout, donation of time and money to the organization of elections, choices in incentivized trust and honesty games, and survey measures of corruption perceptions and democratic values. 18 4.1
The first outcome we study is individual voter turnout.
Voting is of course the primary way citizens participate in representative democracy and hold their representatives accountable.

While a number of existing studies focus on precinct-level outcomes in local elections, through a legal agreement with Mexico’s Electoral Authority, we were able to use administrative data on individual level turnout in the 2021 election for the Federal Congress, which was the first after AMLO’s landslide 2018 win on an anti-corruption platform. 19 Combining individual level randomization and individual turnout data is a precious but rare opportunity.

It affords higher statistical power, allows us to study heterogeneity at the citizen level, and allows us to avoid the numerous problems of ecological inference that can result from using more aggregate precinct-level measures. 20 Further administrative data on individual turnout also has the substantial benefit of allaying concerns about social desirability biases or demander effects that can often plague studies that rely on survey responses to study individual voter decisions (in fact, we are able to measure substantial differences even within our survey).
As mentioned above, ex ante, providing evidence of apex corruption could in principle have effects that could go either way.
On one hand, exposure to evidence of apex corruption could raise turnout as voters seek to hold corrupt politicians accountable by voting for the opposing side.

On the other, insofar as voting reflects support for democracy and formal democratic organizations, beliefs in the fairness of the democratic process, or internalized norms of voting as a civic duty, one might expect apex corruption to reduce political participation and turnout.
Voting may also decrease if the treatment makes the parties less differentiated.

Indeed, we pre-specified our directional hypothesis that the apex corruption treatments “ will diminish support, particularly for the culpable group, but also for the democratic system and trust more broadly (see our pre-analysis plan, pg.3)” We further anticipated “ more updating for the Incumbent video ” as “ while many people think the opposition parties are corrupt, the incumbent party–Morena– is viewed as much less so.” Table 1 provides the basic set of results, pooling both incumbent and opposition apex corruption treatments.

We begin with a basic specification with no controls other than enumerator and municipality fixed effects (Column 1), before controls adding relevant demographic and pre-treatment characteristics (Column 2), and focusing on the urban sample for which we randomized treatment timing (Column 3).

As Columns 1-4 show, apex corruption reduces individual voter turnout by about 4. percentage points 18 The design of the survey experiment aligns well with the recommendations of Stantcheva about making the information in treatments legitimate and trustworthy, introducing incentivized questions, providing limited information about the purpose of the study, monitoring implementation in real time, introducing attention checks, and reducing fatigue by keeping the survey short and engaging.

The order of the modules and the questions within the modules were randomized to avoid order effects. 19 We found 80% of our subjects in the administrative data using the Mexican official person identifier.
The matched percentage and respondents’ characteristics are balanced across arms (see Tables OA-4 and OA-5 ). 20 On June 6th, 2021, Mexicans voted for 500 federal congresspersons (Diputados).
Oaxaquẽnos had to choose 10 of those (with an average of 170K voters in each district) by simple majority and proportional representation.
The intervention is non-partisan, with individuals having an equal chance being exposed to apex corruption by both incumbent and opposition parties.
The treatment group is also so small that the chances of influencing election outcomes are tiny.
Morena won all but one of the 10 districts.
(pp) on average and 6.2pp in the urban sample (corresponding to 6.7% and 10.7% of the mean turnout rate, with p-values of a decrease: 0.066 and 0.043 respectively).

Columns 5-6 further unpacks how these average effects differ according to the timing of treatment, exploiting the fact that, in the urban sample, participants were also randomly assigned to receive the treatment a week before the elections rather than a month before.

The effects of apex corruption lead to a striking 15.6pp decrease in turnout (or 27.1%) when participants were randomly assigned to receive the treatment a week before the elections rather than a month before.

Economic Underperformance treatment: Returning to Table 1, note that, importantly, and in striking contrast to the apex corruption treatments, the economic underperformance treatment increases the probability an individual will turn out to vote (by 8-10pp, or 13%-17%).

Further, as Table OA-9 shows, the economic underperformance video also reduces expressed voter support for the incumbent, by 20.8pp, when the treatment occurs a week before the elections (p-value of a decrease 0.028).

The chief beneficiaries of that treatment are small parties that have not led the government in recent years (which enjoy an increase in expressed voter support of 6.3pp or 262.5%) and ‘null vote or no vote’ protest votes (by about 10pp).

Thus, unlike the apex corruption treatments, evidence of economic and policy underperformance spurs voters’ willingness to turnout and hold politicians accountable, registering protest votes against the ruling parties.

Nation Building treatment: Finally, and contrary to our hypothesis, the nation-building treatment does not raise voter turnout (Table 1 ), nor as we show below, does it increase other pro-democracy behaviors.
Instead, as we will see, it appears to have broadly fostered behaviors that were more aligned with the nationalist populist messaging of the leader of the incumbent government.

As Table OA-9 shows, this includes lowering the votes for the main opposition parties (whose support falls by 8.5pp when treated close to the elections, p-value of a decrease 0.062), largely to the benefit of the incumbent, whose support weakly rises by 4.6pp (p-value 0.25).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We have so far established that apex corruption reduces voter turnout, and particularly does so for the incumbent (perceived to exemplify apex honesty) when treatment occurs close to the elections.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: However, one interpretation of these results is that they may still reflect a form of accountability: that rather than a decline in democratic values, voters who continue to believe in the system are choosing not to turnout as a means to hold the perpetrators of apex corruption accountable by choosing not to vote for their parties specifically.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We now provide direct evidence that apex corruption, particularly by the incumbent, is in fact inducing a broader decline in democratic values, and does so across each of the components—the organizations, beliefs and internalized norms—that underpin the institution of liberal democracy.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We do this both by measuring other natural pro-democratic behaviors as well as through lab-in-the-field experiments.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Natural Behaviors: Volunteering and Donating to Electoral Organizations: We measure support for the formal organizations supporting democracy in two natural ways through which citizens can interact with the system in the real world.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Immediately after the treatments, we provided participants the opportunity to donate either time or money to support the implementation of the elections.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Time: we asked subjects if they would be willing to be official electoral observers.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: This required them to provide their email and voter ID to start the process immediately on the webpage of the electoral authority ( Instituto Nacional Electoral: INE ).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We explained that the short-run cost would be to devote five minutes to be enrolled, and the long-run one would be to undergo a short official training of a few hours online.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We recorded whether they registered.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Volunteering as an electoral observer is a measure of the desire to participate in the democratic process and protect the integrity of voting, and it is a costly action.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Money: we asked subjects if they would be willing to donate 10 pesos to buy water and snacks for poll booth workers, reminding them that these workers were essential to holding elections.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We had a piggy bank where respondents could deposit the money.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Our research team used this money to buy and deliver water and snacks for poll booth workers on election day.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Lab in the Field: Beliefs and Norms.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We now turn to examine the effects of apex corruption on two other components of liberal democratic institutions: beliefs and norms, using lab in the field methodologies.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: As an incentivized measure of how individual’s beliefs in the trustworthiness of political figures and citizens 22 See e.g.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: David Agren, “Mexico bill threatens independence of electoral oversight.”, the Financial Times, Nov 30 2022.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: As discussed above, while trust games can measure citizens beliefs about the trustworthiness of others, apex corruption may also affect internalized norms of honesty. 26 We measure the propensity to steal with impunity by adapting the resource allocation game (RAG, building on Lowes et al. ).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We gave subjects a non-transparent bag that contained 30 coins and an opaque envelope.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: In private, the respondent was asked to choose heads or tails before flipping each coin.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: They were entitled to keep the coin if their guess (unobserved by us) was correct.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: However, they were asked to place the coin inside the envelope if their guess was incorrect.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: After doing this for the 30 coins, the respondent would seal the envelope and give it back to the surveyor.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: In the first instance, envelopes were either randomly labeled “ To the municipal mayor ”–to be allocated for public projects – or “Neighbor”–to be sent to an anonymous neighbor from within the municipality.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We explained that the surveyor would not open the envelope.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We then had the subject repeat the exercise, with the other type of envelope. 27 Because there is no chance of external punishment, following Lowes et al. and Fisman and Miguel, we interpret any stealing as consistent with the weakening of internalized norms among citizens against rule-breaking and theft. 28 As Table 4 shows, on average, participants send 14. out of 30 coins to the municipal mayor and 14. coins to a neighbor.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Importantly, both are very close to the expected value of 1/2 (15 coins) under the null hypothesis of no theft.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Notice that these very similar average propensities to steal from different targets are different from those in the trust games, and are consistent with strong internalized norms of honesty.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: However, when exposed to apex incumbent corruption, citizens send 0. fewer coins (i.e. they ‘steal’ 4% of the mean) to their local (municipal) mayor for public projects, p-value of a decrease 0.024).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Apex incumbent corruption also has a negative impact on honest dealings with neighbors as well (of -0. coins, p-value of a decrease 0.14).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Further, the effect of apex incumbent corruption is particularly pronounced on increasing both types of stealing in the urban sample (Cols 3 and 6).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: In contrast, the opposition corruption treatment does not have a distinguishable effect.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: To test whether increased incentives for opportunism affect theft behavior in each treatment condition, we further advance upon the pioneering work by Lowes et al. by also varying the value of the coins provided, with 10 coins each of 1 peso, 2 peso and 5 peso denominations in each bag.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Table OA-13 shows the effects.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: In the control, on average, participants sent one fewer 5 peso coin to both the public projects bag (4. out of 10) and to neighbors (4. out of 10) relative to the 1 peso coins (5. and 5. 25 Trust in the anonymous politician and anonymous neighbors are also undermined by each of the other treatments as well, though some of the effects are more muted (Table OA-12 ). 26 See, also, for example, Diamond: “ Once ordinary people see that the political class is grabbing public wealth and advantage at every turn, they ask ‘why not me?’ ” 27 We randomized whether the trust game or the stealing game was played first, and within each, we randomized whether the politician/ municipal mayor or the neighbor module was played first.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Because surveyors were handling money we had a supervisor for every five surveyors.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We told surveyors that we would audit a fraction of the surveys.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Finally, the envelopes were such that once sealed they would be torn if somebody tried to open them.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: We do not find a different number of coins by surveyor.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Because the enumerator does not see or know how many coins are in the envelopes, and because a low number of coins is not a guarantee of stealing, it is unlikely that experimenter demand effects drive this result. 28 As mentioned above, important recent work has found that exposure to evidence of corruption by local politicians increases cheating in secondary school level exams and stealing in supermarkets.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Along with our focus on apex rather than local corruption, we further complement these studies in several additional respects.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: First, we do so in a context where citizens cannot be caught cheating, so behavior has to be due to norms internal to the person.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Second, exposure is randomized, ensuring internal validity.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Third, we can test whether behavior depends on the identity of the person from whom the citizen is stealing.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: respectively), suggesting that even though theft is overall low, participants are sensitive to the stakes.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: However, in this dimension, the nation-building treatment did accord with our expectations, reducing this form of opportunistic theft and increasing the willingness of participants to send the most valuable coin to the local mayor for public projects (by about 1/3 of a 5 peso coin).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: The economic underperformance video, in contrast, has the opposite effect on such opportunistic theft, increasing theft of the most valuable coin (again by about 1/3 of a 5 peso coin).

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Similarly, incumbent apex corruption increases theft and has significant effects even for the 1 peso coins, where the incentives for dishonesty are the lowest.

section_name: Effects on Other Pro-Democratic Political Behaviors
text: Thus, though internalized norms can be deep-rooted, having emerged from institutions of the past, apex politicians, particularly those perceived as honest, may be specially positioned to affect norms about honesty, even when the incentives for dishonesty are weak.

So far, we have shown that apex incumbent corruption reduces democracy-supporting behaviors, including voter turnout and support for electoral organizations, as well as reducing trust in politicians and neighbors and enhancing stealing from public projects in incentivized games.
A natural question is whether citizens are consciously making these choices as a reflection of changing democratic values or whether these behaviors reflect unconscious processes and attitudes.
We address two main potential sources of the latter first.

Unconscious Mechanisms: Cognitive Dissonance and Motivated Reasoning Theories of motivated reasoning and cognitive dissonance predict that when partisan attachments are strong, citizens discount negative information about their preferred party or blame negative outcomes on the other side.

If instead, citizens may be driven to support political movements like Morena in large part because they see its leader, AMLO, as exemplifying apex honesty, the effects of revealing apex corruption may instead remain strong.

Given that affective polarization along the Morena vs non-Morena lines is as strong as that of Republicans vs Democrats in the United States, we pre-registered that we would test for differential effects among ex-ante supporters of these parties.
Table OA-10 examines voting behavior among ex ante Morena supporters and non-supporters.
We find evidence against cognitive dissonance/motivated reasoning: revealing incumbent (i.e.

Morena) apex corruption a week before the election has a strong effect among ex ante Morena supporters ( β = -0.232, se=0.105), and reduces their vote for Morena by 24.2pp in favor of no or null votes (which increase by 28.2pp).

Symmetrically, the effects of the opposition (i.e. non-Morena) corruption video are particularly marked among non-Morena supporters, who reduce their reported willingness to vote for the opposition by 29.5pp on average, again mainly in favor of not voting. 29 These effects suggest that apex corruption video revelations are indeed overcoming the potential tendencies to ignore negative information by partisans that processes of motivated reasoning and cognitive dissonance predict. 30 29 Figure OA-8 shows that the differences in voting behaviors we ultimately observe in the election are also mimicked in the voting intentions expressed by participants when surveyed moments after being exposed to the treatment videos.

In particular, once again, ex-ante Morena supporters express a reduced willingness to vote for Morena following the incumbent apex corruption video, while non-Morena supporters exposed to the opposition video are less likely to report their intention to vote for the opposition. 30 Acemoglu et al. similarly find that incumbent supporters do respond to information unfavorable to the incumbent.

Immediate Conscious Changes in Values To instead examine whether the behavioral changes in support for liberal democratic institutions reflect changing perceptions and attitudes of which citizens are consciously aware, we use a battery of survey questions measured immediately after the video treatments.

As we describe below, the use of these survey questions also has the benefit of allowing us to assess whether the effects persist over time and accumulate with multiple scandals, and whether they generalize beyond our Mexican RCT setting to other countries experiencing apex corruption scandals.

In particular, we examine the effects of apex corruption on a set of pre-registered indices based upon a battery of questions on (1) generalized corruption perceptions 31, (2) democratic values 32, and trust in (3) democratic institutions 33 and (4) fellow citizens. 34 Figure 2: Effects of Apex Corruption On Immediate Conscious Perceptions and Values (a) Pooled Apex Corruption 0.225 (P < 0.001) -0.160 (P < 0.001) -0.192 (P < 0.001) -0.116 (P =0.002) Corruption Index Democracy Index Trust Inst.
increase their perceptions of broader corruption by 0.225 σ (p-value<0.001).
That is, unlike a number of important studies, the apex corruption treatment did significantly change conscious perceptions of broader corruption.

All components of the corruption index are affected, with treated individuals revealing a 6% decrease in the belief that there has been progress reducing corruption decreases, a 5% increase in the share of politicians they believe are corrupt, a 4% increase in the share of taxes stolen, and an increase of 12.4pp (or 4%) in the belief that all politicians are corrupt (Table OA-16 ). 35 Further, the apex corruption treatments immediately undermine the index of conscious democratic support by 0. σ (p-value<0.001).

Again, all components of the index move in the direction of lower conscious support for democracy: satisfaction with democracy decreases by 15%, 4. pp fewer people (or 5.4%) claim that democracy is the best form of government, and 3. pp fewer people (or 4.6%) consider it essential to live in a democracy (Table OA-17 ). 36 Thus, the voting behaviors and willingness to donate to democratic institutions above finds resonance in immediate survey indicators of conscious democratic support.

Beyond this, and again paralleling the lab in the field behaviors above, apex corruption leads conscious trust in democratic office-holders and formal institutions to also immediately decrease, relative to control, by 0. σ (p-value<0.001).
This again shows up in all components of the index: trust in the President decreases by 5.6pp (or 10%), in Congress by 5.8pp (or 23%), and in political parties by 2.4pp (or 20%).
Even the media is affected, with its trustworthiness decreasing by 4.8pp (or 15%, see Table OA-18 ).

Further, the corrosive effect of apex corruption spills over not just to the media, it also reduces trust in other citizens by 0.116 %) in individual’s expressed belief that it is essential to live in a democracy (Table OA-17 ).

Further, like the apex corruption treatment, the economic underperformance/ mismanagement treatment also increases general corruption perceptions (Table OA-14 ), particularly by reducing respondents’ 35 By focusing on pre-registered indices, we already address potential concerns about multiple hypothesis testing.

Fore completeness, we also report false discovery rate (FDR) q-values in Appendix B. Typically one does not report q-values across families of outcomes, nonetheless, for the interested reader, we provide these in Table OA-15 as well. 36 In terms of effect sizes, the effects of the three-minute apex corruption treatments on reducing the support for democracy are about twice as large as those of the strongest of the eight-minute video treatments found to have a backlash effect on support for democratic practices among an internet panel of US partisans found in Voelkel et al. 37 The apex corruption videos could affect citizens who already knew this footage, with the videos acting as a reminder.
But most likely, the videos mostly affect those who have not seen them.
While this is testable, conducting this test is complicated by the fact that we cannot ask the control group whether they had seen the videos (as they were not shown to them).

We can, however, (within the corruption video arms) interact the indicator for being exposed to the corruption video with an indicator for knowing them, while trying to control for predictors of awareness selected by Lasso.
By doing this, we find that the effects on corruption, democracy, and trust in institutions come mainly from those who report being ex-ante unaware of the corruption videos (see Table OA-20 ).
perceptions that progress has been made fighting corruption and increasing the share of politicians they believe to be corrupt (Table OA-16 ).
Self-reported trust in politicians, like the President and the Congress, also fall (Table OA-18 ) in response to this treatment, though there are no spillovers to trust in other citizens.

Further, like apex corruption, the economic underperformance immediately reduces individuals willingness to voice support for democracy, reflecting a fall in democratic satisfaction and a questioning of whether living in a democracy is essential (Table OA-17 ).

We interpret these results as confirming that policy ineptitude and economic underperformance provides a parallel, and complementary channel to apex corruption in increasing general corruption perceptions and lowering trust in the probity of democratic officeholders.
However, as we have seen, there are also important differences in its ultimate effects on actual behaviors.

In striking contrast to apex corruption, the ultimate behavioral effects of randomized revelations of economic underperformance and mismanagement are to spur voter turnout and accountability, with much more limited spillovers to other natural and incentivized pro-democratic behaviors.
Persistent Conscious Effects on Democratic Values.
Four months after the treatment, for the city of Oaxaca, we re-surveyed our sample.
We paid special attention to ensure that the same person was being re-interviewed by going to the same address and the respondent having the same gender, age, and name.

This allows us to test for persistence by using the original randomization and measuring support for democracy at the beginning of the follow-up survey using the same index measures. 38 We achieved a 77% (non-differential) response rate.
Table OA-8 in the Appendix shows no differential attrition for the follow-up survey.
As Figure 3 shows, the apex corruption treatment and control groups had largely converged in terms of their broader corruption perceptions four months after treatment.

Nevertheless, the effect on lowered professed support for democracy persists for both apex corruption treatments (an average effect of -0. σ, with one-sided p-value = 0.027). 39 Testing a Second Potential Remedy: The Effects of Common Financial Exposures As described above, instead of priming common identity, as in the nation-building treatment, after collecting the immediate survey outcomes, we provided exposure to a subset to the broad Mexican index fund, and thus a means to share in and learn about the gains of the common good—and common risks— of an important component of the national economy. 40 Treated individuals received 200 pesos (about $ 10) of financial assets tracking the broad S&P BMV IPC Mexican stock index, receiving text messages allowing them to trade up to 25 pesos each week for three-four weeks.
Takeup was relatively 38 We see this is as a meaningful contribution, in and of itself.

Because of the difficulties of resurveying the same respondents, measuring persistence has eluded some of the most important survey experiments in the formation of social preferences. 39 The effects are 0. % of their portfolio, the incentives were much more stark and binary: respondents had to register a decision each of four possible opportunities in order to receive the value of their portfolio at the end.

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: While planning fieldwork for the follow-up survey, a second brother of the incumbent president was caught taking bribes on film.

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: This allowed us to create an additional 3-minute corruption video we refer to as the Two Brothers Corruption treatment (henceforth CI 2 ).

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: This video features sequential footage of bribes being exchanged not just by one but by two siblings of the president. 42 Therefore, after measuring the persistence of the effects from the previous videos, we randomized half of the follow-up (Oaxaca city) sample to receive the Two Brothers corruption video, stratifying by the treatment arms of the first experiment. 43 We are able to measure two different types of accumulation.

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: First, by comparing the half of the control group against the half that got the new CI 2 video, we can estimate the effect of the two-brother video.

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: We can then benchmark the size of this effect against that of the one -brother CI video we estimated above, and ask whether a two-in-one treatment showing evidence for corrupt acts involving two brothers has a larger effect than a similar video showing evidence for just one.

section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
text: Further, we can also examine if there is an accumulating two separate doses effect: i.e., whether people who receive two doses of evidence of apex corruption—one in the first round of the experiment and a second in this round—-suffer larger decreases in support for democracy than those who just got one, i.e., we can test if we can reject the hypothesis − 0. 23 σ. This decline in immediate expressed support for democracy is about 40 percent larger than the − 0. 159 % larger than the effect of just the two-in-one video ( CI 2 ) and 137% larger than the original corruption incumbent ( CI ) videos.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: Our randomized control trial has helped us establish that apex corruption revelations undermine support for liberal democratic institutions, measured using administrative individual turnout data, natural and incentivized behaviors, and survey measures of values and attitudes.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: A natural question is whether this key pattern generalizes beyond the Mexican context and whether it can help explain part of the broader macro patterns in democratic backsliding that we observe.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: To establish this, we can exploit the fact that representative surveys, such as Latinobarometer, have happened to be fielded just before and just after the breaking of apex corruption revelations ‘in the wild’. 45 In particular, we use large-scale survey data combined with the exact timing of apex corruption revelations in 17 Latin American countries from 2008 to 2018 to estimate the impact of apex corruption.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: Data on Apex Corruption Revelations.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: To identify the exact timing of each corruption scandal, we scraped the Twitter news feeds of the 4 major news outlets in each of 17 Latin American countries for the period 2008–2018 (see Table OA-28 for a list) searching for words related to corruption, such as "corrupt," "corruption," "bribery," and "illicit enrichment", among others.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: We supplemented this with news reports from the LexisNexis Spanish newspapers database using the same words during the same time period.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: This resulted in 142,000+ news-tweets and 10,000+ LexisNexis news reports for all countries and years.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: Because we want to focus on apex corruption, we define an event to be a ‘apex corruption scandal’ if it satisfies all of the following conditions: (i) The news event had to be covered by at least two news channels in the country on the same day; (ii) those involved in the scandal had to be high-ranked politicians such as presidents, former presidents, ministers, former ministers, opposition leaders, governors, federal congressmen, high ranked federal directors or federal judges; (iii) the news event had to be a scoop, that is, the scandal had to be a new scandal and not just continued reportage on an existing one.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
text: We identified 176 scandal outbreaks over the period that satisfied our apex corruption definition.

section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America

However, because the apex corruption revelations are entirely divorced from the implementation of the surveys, one advantage of the cross-scandal survey approach that is shared with the administrative turnout analysis in the RCT is that it too is not vulnerable to biases due to experimenter demand effects or social desirability concerns.

section_name: Effects of Apex Corruption Across Scandals in Latin America
text: Instead the effects of apex corruption in undermining support for liberal democracy and to increase the desire for an authoritarian alternative appear to generalize.
Scandals implicating those at the very highest levels of government in democratic states in corrupt activities have been all-too-common, even while support for democratic institutions declines.

In this paper, combining evidence both from ’apex corruption in the wild’ and a randomized control trial, we show that such apex corruption erodes democratic values and support for democratic institutions, reduces citizens’ vote turnout and their engagement in costly activities in support of democratic organizations.
Apex corruption also appears to undermine citizens’ internalized norms of honesty and trust in democratic institutions and in one another.
Furthermore, we find that the effects on democratic values persist over time and accumulate in the face of further evidence of corruption.

Thus, while it may seem at first that disclosure of apex corruption is positive for democratic institutions, enhancing transparency and accountability, there appears to be a darker side to disclosing apex corruption: an erosion of democratic values. 60 Our paper points to potential paths forward.

While our intervention priming common national identity proved not to enhance broad support for democracy, our intervention providing equity in the broader economy through common financial exposures holds some promise.

Further, just as corrupt acts by highly-placed individuals can have broad negative impacts, there is also a possibility that evidence of apex honesty by high-profile leaders can rebuild societal trust and support for democracy.
Our paper raises several important questions for future research.
First, could evidence of apex honesty be effective for reducing corruption and building trust?
Second, are the effects of apex corruption similar in authoritarian regimes?

Do prominent displays of judicial enforcement and punishment of those implicated in apex corruption scandals serve to restore trust in the system? 61 Finally, which policies could counteract the negative impacts of apex corruption and help boost democratic values?
With support for democracy eroding to worrying levels around the world, the need to better understand how to counteract the effects of apex corruption is great indeed.

Indeed in our follow-on paper, González-Téllez et al., we find that citizens in places exposed to apex corruption are more likely to resort to violence rather than relying on the democratic process to settle disagreements. 61 Sample size limitations and the fact that many scandals go unpunished and take many years in court limited such a test in this paper.
